Therefore, when `__lapb_remove_cb()` runs, the refcount is exactly 2, so its internal `lapb_put()` only drops the list reference from 2 to 1 and cannot free the object. This is also KUnit test code, not a production attacker-reachable path, so it does not create a real-world crashable or exploitable UAF.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "`net/mac80211/tests/chan-mode.c:249-255` frees `elems` only when `!IS_ERR_OR_NULL(elems)`, then checks `IS_ERR(elems)` and `PTR_ERR(elems)`. `include/linux/err.h:55-57` defines `PTR_ERR(ptr)` as `return (long) ptr;`, and `include/linux/err.h:68-70` defines `IS_ERR(ptr)` as `return IS_ERR_VALUE((unsigned long)ptr);`; neither reads from `*elems`, so even a stale pointer value here is not a freed-memory dereference. The two `.error = EINVAL` cases are `net/mac80211/tests/chan-mode.c:51-54` and `130-134`. For error cases, `ieee80211_determine_chan_mode()` rejects unsupported required rates/selectors at `net/mac80211/mlme.c:1117-1122` and returns `ERR_PTR(ret)` at `1226-1228`.   "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported source/sink pair is superficially plausible (`nr_neigh_put()` at `net/netrom/nr_route.c:246` followed by dereference at line 248), but the free assumption is wrong on this path. The old object in `nr_node->routes[2].neighbour` still has a persistent list reference when line 246 executes, so dropping the route's reference cannot free it. In this subsystem, `nr_neigh->count` is only the logical number of routes using the neighbour; the actual lifetime is controlled by `refcount`. Each route insertion takes an extra `nr_neigh_hold()`, while list membership itself owns a base reference. Therefore line 246 only releases the route-held reference, and line 248 is the intended post-decrement check that decides whether to remove the neighbour from the global list and drop the final list reference. The CSA path is infeasible because it models `nr_neigh_put()` as if it could immediately free this object without accounting for the project-wide ownership/refcount invariant. This is a false positive. Separately, the flagged branch is not on the remote packet-learning path: `nr_route_frame()` calls `nr_add_node(..., quality = 0)`, while the report requires `quality != 0`, so the branch is practically reachable only from privileged routing ioctls.",
  "FP_Reason": ["A1"],
  "evidence": "1. `include/net/netrom.h:137-143` shows `nr_neigh_put()` frees only when `refcount_dec_and_test()` reaches zero.\\n2. `net/netrom/nr_route.c:392-418` (`nr_add_neigh`) initializes `refcount` to 1 and inserts the object into `nr_neigh_list` without an immediate put; this demonstrates a persistent list-owned reference.\\n3. `net/netrom/nr_route.c:172-179` (`nr_add_node` new-neighbour path) adds the neighbour to the list and then does `nr_neigh_hold(nr_neigh)`, meaning there is both a list ref and a temporary/local ref.\\n4. Every route that stores a neighbour takes another ref: `net/netrom/nr_route.c:203-204`, `240-241`, and `255-256` all do `nr_neigh_hold(nr_neigh); nr_neigh->count++;`. Thus any neighbour currently stored in `nr_node->routes[2].neighbour` has at least the list ref plus this route ref before line 246.\\n5. The reported block at `net/netrom/nr_route.c:245-249` does `count--`, then `nr_neigh_put(old)`, then checks `if (old->count == 0 && !old->locked) nr_remove_neigh(old);`. This ordering only works if `old` remains alive after the put, which it does because the list reference still exists. `nr_remove_neigh()` at `net/netrom/nr_route.c:305-309` is the operation that drops that list reference.\\n6. Reachability is also limited: `net/netrom/af_netrom.c:1249-1254` gates route add/delete ioctls behind `CAP_NET_ADMIN`, and `net/netrom/nr_route.c:768-770` shows the packet-driven path calls `nr_add_node()` with `quality = 0`, so the report's `quality != 0` branch is not remotely triggerable through normal route learning.",
